passage: A public health study tracked hospital readmission rates for patients who participated in post-discharge follow-up programs compared to those who did not. The researchers contend that structured follow-up care is most beneficial for patients with chronic conditions, producing a larger reduction in readmissions for this group than for patients without chronic conditions.
stem: Which choice most effectively uses data from the figure to support the researchers' contention?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Among chronic-condition patients, those in follow-up programs had a 12% readmission rate versus 31% for non-participants (a 19-point reduction), while among non-chronic patients, participants had a 7% rate versus 14% for non-participants (a 7-point reduction).

choices.label: B
choices.text: Non-chronic patients in follow-up programs had a readmission rate of 7%, which was lower than the 12% rate for chronic-condition patients in the same programs.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Chronic-condition patients who did not participate in follow-up programs had a 31% readmission rate, the highest of any group in the study.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Follow-up program participants had lower readmission rates than non-participants in both the chronic group (12% vs. 31%) and non-chronic group (7% vs. 14%).

explanation: The claim is that follow-up care produces a LARGER reduction for chronic-condition patients than for non-chronic patients. Choice A quantifies the reduction for both groups (19 points vs. 7 points), directly showing the chronic group benefits more. Choice B compares the two program-participant groups but doesn't address the magnitude of reduction. Choice C only highlights one statistic. Choice D shows both groups benefit but doesn't compare the magnitude of the reductions.

distractorRationale.A: Correct: calculates and compares the readmission reduction for both patient groups, showing the chronic group's 19-point drop exceeds the non-chronic group's 7-point drop.
distractorRationale.B: Wrong (wrong comparison): compares chronic vs. non-chronic patients within the follow-up group, showing non-chronic patients have lower rates overall, which doesn't address the relative benefit of the program.
distractorRationale.C: Wrong (incomplete evidence): cites only the highest readmission rate without comparing it to the follow-up group or to non-chronic outcomes.
distractorRationale.D: Wrong (incomplete evidence): shows the program helps both groups but fails to compare the magnitude of reduction, which is the specific claim being tested.

imageDescription: Grouped bar chart titled 'Hospital Readmission Rates by Patient Type and Follow-Up Status.' X-axis: Patient Type (Chronic Conditions, Non-Chronic). Two bars per category: Follow-Up Program (green) and No Follow-Up (red). Chronic Conditions: Follow-Up = 12%, No Follow-Up = 31%. Non-Chronic: Follow-Up = 7%, No Follow-Up = 14%. Source: National Health Services Research Institute, 2024.

chartData.title: Hospital Readmission Rates by Patient Type and Follow-Up Status

passageMetadata.source: Adapted from National Health Services Research Institute outcomes study
